speaker: interviewer
text: Three, Mr. Ignac Feldman. All this time, Mr. Feldman, had you had any word from your wife at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm in this-- of Lwow. Nowhere a hotel or a motel where to go to sleep. But OK, from the farmers what give me a piece of bread. Till I came, I have a piece of bread. Then-- then I see where to sleep at night. Then I see a jail. Then I go in the jail. And I tell, listen, they ask me who I are. I tell them, listen. I am from the city of Lodz. They tell, OK, the jail is open. You have a mattress. You can sleep. I sleep over and after there, I need work. Then working in the-- in the city, I see street cars. I put this together. I go there where the shops and the street cars is, and I come there and I tell, listen, I am a mechanic. I can work street cars, painting. Right away from the place, yes, you are in good luck. And yes, and they give me right away. And they ask me, look at the ration already. If you need bread or other thing, come in the restaurant. You will eat to give you a few ruble.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they look at me. Still I have swollen legs from walking so far. Then you know what? We will give you money, rest up two days, and then come. After two days, come start working. We trust you. Give the name and everything, you are an honest man. And they give me a uniform. It's from the form the-- working-- from the street car workers. They have a special uniform. They give me the uniform. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I worked there. And after a few days, I decided to go back when I'm in civil already. More with energy to go back to Lodz to my wife. After all, I love my wife. She is kind. There are no [INAUDIBLE]. Nothing divided us between border Russia and Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: I come on the train one evening. I don't know. I was looking two young gentlemen observe me. I thought they are from the security. Why they look like that? Then one comes to me and they ask me, is your brother [INAUDIBLE]? My wife's brother. I-- I told them, yes, why you ask? Where are you going? I tell them, I'm going back-- if you know my brother-in-law, then he must know my wife. I go back to my wife to bring her over. Then you will not go because you are [INAUDIBLE] and we know about you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You stay here. We go to Lodz and we give everything what you ask, and you will bring-- we'll bring them over. And I don't like them, but they pushed me down from the train. And they, listen, we are not workers on this. We go because the Germans will shoot you right away in the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these men?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was good friends from my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Again, no. Then I mark to them fast to my wife leave everything, I not need anything. Come over with your brother, with your father. And leave it because I'm afraid if she will watch the furniture and everything, then she will miss-- she will miss the train, you understand me?

speaker: interviewer
text: And so then-- then I was-- yes, then I was, meantime, when I worked by the street cars, and I make nice. Then the master tell them, then he's a very good specialist come and covered their-- and they asked who is [INAUDIBLE], the young man. They said, you know what? We like you to finish for us the autos to repair. You will work for us. Nobody [INAUDIBLE]. OK, I was working.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day, the [INAUDIBLE] they call me. And when they called me up, I was afraid. You know, when they call you, you can be on the Siberia or [INAUDIBLE] or I don't know where. When I come in, right away it was-- he give me a cigarette.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, yes. Sit down. Take a sit. Then they ask me, who you leave in Lodz? Then I give him my mother, my sisters, my brother, my wife. He look at me. Your wife? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tomorrow, he tell me, work till 12 o'clock. After 12 o'clock, take your free because your wife will come over. She is arrested on the-- on the border and everything. I called now to let her be free. So tomorrow, you will meet your wife. And so was it. And when they different give her the regard, she took her father and her brother and she comes over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your meeting.

speaker: interviewee
text: The meeting was-- I will tell it. You know, before comes I meet her father because when she goes with her father, with her brother, with a friend, they was not together in one group. But if the Germans-- a Jew not supposed to travel with the-- with the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: If a German notice one, at least one will be killed, not everybody. So they was divided in different train. But I didn't know that because her father comes first to me. Then I grab him. Where is Lonia? And I tell him, I don't know. She was supposed to go. What was happened? She went from border to border over, and he smuggled the border, you know? Then he comes faster. And after-- after then, I met Lonia. So we was working. As a worker, I have very good there. I cannot tell them bad. OK. You must keep your mouth shut like a dictatorship but the economical was-- was good. I have-- my wife comes over. Then they give us a-- a room for a family. You see? What year-- when was this exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was already in November when my wife comes over.

speaker: interviewer
text: November '40?

speaker: interviewer
text: '39, still.

speaker: interviewee
text: '39. November '39, my wife comes over because I think they hanged a few Jews in Lodz on the public--

speaker: interviewer
text: The public square?

speaker: interviewee
text: The public square. So the name is Balutski Rinnek, Balutski Square. And so she decided to-- to-- to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much were you getting paid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? By the-- I cannot-- this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can't remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot remember, you see? But still, you see the year, then we were-- and then my wife was-- and so we wait. And I was thinking, OK, now it's time for a baby to have, and my wife was pregnant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide that this was a good time to have a baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I think, you see, I was in the war and I know if I will go in the war, at least somebody will be after me, a sentiment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then comes close-- then I decided when she's pregnant, no, this isn't the place to be. I make a nice living in the city of Lwow-- of [? Lanberg ?] but it is good to go in deep Russia. I was afraid the Germans will attack Russia. That was the feelings. This is 1939, the end of 1939. OK, there was fighting with England. But I was thinking, I have my mother's sister in England in the city of Bradford. If I will go to the other side near China, in Mongolia, I can come close to India. And if I come close to India, maybe I will come to India, to England with my family. Because I know in-- in Lodz, the family will be wiped out. I have nothing to think will be no-- even not-- nobody knows what will be, but my feeling was like that. And I give a detail to my wife about Bradford 77. This is the city where my-- my mother's sister live.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name? Pisarek. Her-- Gitel. Her name was Goldfarb, but they shortened that in Gold or Golden, you see? Like that shortened in England the name. But it was like very nice-- like you tell Eaton or so, everybody knows in the city. So Bradford 77, this was the confidence and this is it. But my wife tells me, no. In [? Lanberg, ?] we have companions. Again, people who run from Lodz. We have the friends and the companions and the-- the clubs. And we have a nice living. Why to go away? I tell her, listen. Here is a war. No, no. And then I explained, listen, Lanka, I know here will be, if the Germans will attack, it's not only the Germans will kill you. The Ukrainians will kill you, too. [INAUDIBLE]. Then I think not one Jewish man can be saved on this territory because I must look at it like that. And she tell me, here is the Russian Army. I tell, listen, I saw the German Army. I saw the Russian Army. The Russian Army cannot keep the front. Maybe they will keep because the distance, the time, the winter, and they have time to organize fresh armies from Siberia, from [INAUDIBLE]. But in beginning, I tell her, the Russian will not get the front. They must back-- step back, back and back. And if you like to stay, listen to me, you know me. I know Ukrainian. I could be saved. But I will not let you, as a pregnant woman, for the Germans to die. And I will say, if you like, I will be dead because you do not understand this situation. It's up to you. Then you take my life in your hand. Then she start crying. Tell, OK, let's go. And we go to the city Stalinabad, in middle Asia. This is not far between Iran and China, you know, very far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were-- I go-- these are the-- how is that-- organized workers who specialists to Asia. And I, with my own sign--

speaker: interviewer
text: Volunteered. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Volunteered with my wife to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I come there, I start working either by finishing furniture, other things. And they tell me, you know you are young man. You go-- you will work in the brigade. They have brigade to work. I tell them, no. I like for myself to work because I didn't know, the law is how much I will make, you will pay me. The first two weeks when they pay me, they tell me, you know, you make so much, like two Russian engineers. You are so fast and so good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And piece work, how you can that make? I tell, this is my trade, and I can close my eyes and do that. And they tell me-- they give me special privileges. Take the brigade, no. I can teach the brigade but I will not take it because I-- I am always afraid because I know me, and I don't like to give directive to the Russian who is their citizens. And I come fresh and tell them to do that. I don't like it. Then I can stay after work and teach your workers how to work? But I will not take over the brigade. Once comes a commission for Moscow, and they ask me, who make the samples what you send? They tell me they was thinking, it's oh, that old man when they call me up. They ask me, have you-- they give you something for the samples what you make? I tell, yes, they pay me. I told them clear what I make. No, a special note was given me privileges especially. You see, to buy a suit or shoes. You not stay in-- in the line. You go to the factory. Give the paper and they make for you, you see, other-- like other privileges they give you, as a good worker, as a good specialist, so we have good-- but takes a few months.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife comes in eight months, pregnant. Then she start crying. We receive letters from [? Lanberg ?] Then othr friends, they go again with vacation in the clubs and everything. And here we are in Asia, no no social life and to go back. And she's crying. I think, Lonia, no, I will not fight. You are pregnant, the last month pregnant. We'll go back. But you must have a paper to go back when you are free from-- from where you are working.

speaker: interviewer
text: They tell me, what? You are crazy. You have so many privileges. But still we are going. We sit on the train going back through-- yes. But now we must go to the Caspian Sea, you see, to-- to come back. And then she cannot go on the boat because in this condition. Then I take off my-- my coat, a raincoat, and I put on her. Then nobody can see she is pregnant. So we come. When she take off in the sea, we are middle of the sea. The captain from the boat give her look, how you came here? Who let you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take her right away to give her a-- a special room. As a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as a woman pregnant, you get special care. And then we go down from the boat. We start with the train to go in direction from Lwow. When we're sitting also in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, Mr. Feldman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were sitting in the train. There was a high-ranking officer was looking on us. And maybe he noticed that I am Jewish with my wife. And he asked me a simple question, not to me. He read a paper, and he opened only his eyes. His mouth, I cannot see. His nose, I cannot see. Only his eyes. He asked me a simple question, [GERMAN]? This mean, are you Jewish?

speaker: interviewer
text: If I am not Jew, I not understand. I will not answer. And nobody will know what he said. If Jewish, then I tell him yes. And they ask me in Jewish, where you are going? We tell him, we are going back to Lwow. He tell me, you know, the Soviet government arrest everybody from you and sent to Siberia. He said, you will not know where your wife is, where the child is born, and you will not meet each other. Go down from the train. Don't go in there. Then he took his belonging, he covered his face. After, when I was in the army, I know this was a general.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my wife was sitting like that. I  tell, listen, I take his words in consideration. And the next station was a big city, Rostov. And then here, we got down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then Rostov, then where to go again? I go again to park-- to the street car, to the [GERMAN]. And I tell listen, I am so and so a mechanic. I'd like to work here. But where do you leave? I tell them I come only to the city. I said, listen, it's very bad. But don't go to the [INAUDIBLE]. Then you live here, make us a sample. Then we will give you papers and we need you, maybe they will let you stay in the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did that. In 10 days, I readied the samples. They paid me very good, gave me papers. From 1917 not till now they have not had a specialist like that. And they ask the organization, the city hall, to let me stay in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: I come there, sit a colonel. He look at me. He tell us, it's a hard problem. You cannot stay here. I tell him where else to go? Where you like to go? I don't how I can go. My wife is now, any day she must give birth. And he tell me, you know, put-- put your wife in the hospital. And in two hours, go out from the city. I thought, what will be? No family, no friends. Where I don't know what has happened with her, with the baby? Then he give me the answer. They have no sentiment for life. He give me the answer, where you will be will be alive, will be alive because you are young. This, I can't take anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I forget myself, where I am. I tell him, listen. In my whole life, I met the first time a bureaucrat like you who not take in consideration a human being's life. I call him a bureaucrat. And bang, he arrest me straight from place. When they bring me to-- to close me in where, then I show him my-- my wife. Downstairs was the doctor from our paper. Go there. And she had the papers, and I was in the papers as [RUSSIAN], so and so, so and so. She went down there. I was sitting two hours. And then her doctor's there and told-- told them I'm not a Soviet citizen. I didn't-- didn't know the law and only a honest man. So he bring me. He called me out back and tell me, listen, we're taking some consideration you are a foreigner and you are young and a good specialist. Take your wife and go to this and this city. Tell him, listen, when he tell him the city, Krasnodar. I tell him this is [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me yes. Why? I tell him the Cossacks. He tells me, yes. I tell him, listen, citizen, you know, I am Jewish. How I will be living with the Cossacks? Then he's like, how you know this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell him, I read the literature from [INAUDIBLE], Michael [INAUDIBLE] about the Cossacks in the time of the civil war. So I know that. Yes, you know. So young, you know even a foreigner. You know it. Yes. OK, he tell me. OK. You can have bread, everything. But go. And matter of fact, you will meet people now from Poland is living in this city. There was a lot Jewish population. It is not 1937. It is now 1940, and we go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where exactly was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krasnodar. I come there. I start working it's like only from autos, painting autos. I put my wife in the hospital, and my son was born there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son's name is Anatol. He was born the 19th of August, 1940. And the life was-- was good. I know what has happened to my family. I know nobody will survive, even--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I know what the Germans will do. I know even before the-- I don't know it was going to be gas-- gas chambers, how they will do or just how-- I know they will liquidate the Jewish population. I know the German propaganda, and I know how sure there was with them 1939, how sure what they will do everything, you see? And the [GERMAN], the [GERMAN]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Superman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Superman. The would do-- other nations had not-- not law, anything, only according I see they will leave the Slavic nation so much how much need the German economy. They will control even the population, how many they need. And it-- it-- it will be no life. And I know my family, nobody will-- will survive. But it was good. And then the Germans attacked Russia. They went, I think, June. the-- the 21st of June or 22. The 22nd of June, they attack Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they come, like I was telling to my wife, the Russians will not keep the front. In two months, they come. And every day, every day, the Russians retreat, retreat. This city, this city. Rostov, Smolensk. You see, Lwow. It is-- it is-- you are in panic, what will be. But again, I tell you know, let's go back to Asia, from where we came, with-- with the baby. It's not so-- it's not so we keep-- and then the bombardment from the-- and then the Russian mobilizes me again.

speaker: interviewer
text: But my wife goes there to the [INAUDIBLE]. Tell, listen, he is not a Soviet citizen. And we'd like to evacuate more to-- to the rear. And I'm with a child, with a baby, what is to do? I don't know what.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one woman so good to her, and she tell, before they cut my hair, and says, give her a paper. They let me free. She took out her wedding ring and she give it away for thankfulness. But once, they call me out and they give me the papers to go out. And I am with my wife and they come for us. Don't turn around. They can change their mind, and we go back. We go back. Then I was afraid to go on the [INAUDIBLE] where we was living until we go to France because the Cossacks know that they can come again to mobilize me. And this was the next day. We take a-- a train and we go more east. We go more east. In [INAUDIBLE], the train stopped. This is the end. They bring us and no more trains. They take the trains they need for the army, because it is war. And we go to a kolkhoz, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is a kolkhoz?

speaker: interviewee
text: A collective farm, a collective farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this, exactly? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, This is not in Caucasus Mountains. [INAUDIBLE]. And the kolkhoz was [GERMAN]. What is the [GERMAN]? It was a German collective farm. But when the war break out, the Russian took out the Germans. They send them all to the-- to Siberia. And now comes people who run from the-- from the firm, they give them to run the farm. But I see there is no organization. You can work and work. And you have a child, the child-- till they give you to eat, the child can die from hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't go to work and my wife tell me, what is not going to work? You will not have the ration. I tell her, OK, it's a piece of bread. I will see. I will.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I look and I see fire, where-- 15 kilometer from there was another small [INAUDIBLE]. This is not a town. Maybe 75 homes that's all. And I see from every home is smoke. This mean people are living, the Cossacks. And I go there and I talk with the manager there. I like to work, but I like my child, my wife, have something to eat. He tell me, but you never work in the farm. I tell, I was not going to my university to take a [NON-ENGLISH]. If you will take me calculus, ask me calculus, I must go to university, but to work in a farm, I not need to graduate university. He start laughing when I mentioned calculus and [NON-ENGLISH]. He tells me, listen, maybe if other-- other men like you, who like algebra because we need people who like to work. And you have everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: I tell, yes. He give me-- what you need? He give me right away bread, milk, and butter. And go there what you need, how much you can take. Go give your wife and your child to eat. Like I bring a million dollars to my wife, the child. In the evening, I come back. They give me horses, wagon and take my children down to the other friend also. We go to the Cossacks. And the Cossacks, there we were staying. And just it was good people. Because we live in-- I tell to myself, if a Cossack can do, I can do. If he can jump over a horse, I will jump the same thing, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am [INAUDIBLE] so whatever. He can work, I can work. And they tell me-- they give me-- after-- after two months, they give me [INAUDIBLE]. You see, as a good worker, as a good farmer. Good till the Germans come close. The Germans come close even there. It's time to run.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was also-- this was also in-- in June, 1942. The German come very close. In July, 1942, they mobilized me.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Red Army Then I tell, listen, I am not a Soviet citizen. And said, we know that. Look, you know this and this kind of dynamite. You know the German dynamite. You speak Russian. You speak German. You speak Polish. We know what you did in the Polish Army. You was fighting. We need you. If we need you, this is the order from the front. No more civil orders. Then you will go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what is with my wife, with my child? Tell, she will be evacuated to deep Asia, and you will go. We know it's not right to do that. But be honest, if you will be killed, it's nothing to talk. If you will stay alive after the war, we will see you, what we have done. But meantime, you will go. Can I stop you there for a moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
